comments.author: Alexander Terp
comments.body:
Fixed (2026-06-14). Root cause: an out-of-range int literal reached rts.convertInt, which panics - and on the interpret path that conversion runs outside any recovery, so it surfaced as a raw Go panic.

Fix: added RAD40015 (ErrIntLiteralOutOfRange) + a CST-level checker pass (addIntLiteralOutOfRangeErrors in rts/check/check.go) that flags int literals failing ParseInt with strconv.ErrRange. validateSyntax surfaces it as a clean error before the panicking conversion. Left the convertInt panic in place as the converter's documented 'valid CST' invariant (now unreachable on the CLI path).

Notes:
- MinInt64 (-9223372036854775808) still can't be written as a literal (the magnitude overflows on its own before the unary minus); now a clean RAD40015 with a suggestion pointing at the '-...807 - 1' workaround, instead of a panic.
- Collateral: core/error_docs/20017.md's example was itself relying on this bug (its literal would have panicked, and int() doesn't even raise RAD20017) - re-based it on round(x, -2), which genuinely demonstrates RAD20017.
- Tests: snapshot core/testing/snapshots/errors/validation/int_literal_out_of_range.snap; error-doc 40015.md + tolerance.

creator: Alexander Terp
description:
RE-VERIFIED 2026-06-14 (rad v0.10.1): still a raw Go panic.

Writing an integer literal that exceeds int64 range causes an unrecoverable Go panic instead of a user-friendly error.

**Repro:**
x = 9223372036854775808   // max int64 + 1
// panic: converter: failed to parse int "9223372036854775808": strconv.ParseInt: ... value out of range

Also cannot express the minimum int64 value as a literal: -9223372036854775808 panics.

**Source:** `rts/converter.go:1212` (was cited as :1151) - `convertInt()` calls `panic()` on parse failure.

**Fix (slam dunk):** replace the `panic()` with a clean rad compile error; the converter already emits errors elsewhere, so callers handle it. Kills a raw Go panic.
title: Int literal overflow causes Go panic
